beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. An article lists a century of confident expert predictions, from 1925 to 2026, that a new technology had no future. The pattern repeats: credentialed insiders dismiss streaming, cloud, or home video, and each technology then reshapes the economy. These were not careless people; their deep grasp of the current business was exactly what blinded them. The better you understand today's paradigm, the harder it becomes to see the one replacing it.

artifactLines: An article lists a century of confident expert predictions, from 1925 to 2026, that a new technology had no future.
The pattern repeats: credentialed insiders dismiss streaming, cloud, or home video, and each technology then reshapes the economy.
These were not careless people; their deep grasp of the current business was exactly what blinded them.
The better you understand today's paradigm, the harder it becomes to see the one replacing it.

beats.narration_text: Those quotes from Keyes, Ellison, and Valenti all share the same trap — expertise from inside the domain. I want to pressure-test whether I'd have made the same call. Take today's loudest 'AI will fail' argument and (1) identify which specific domain expertise is fueling it, (2) show me how that same expertise would have made Blockbuster's CEO dismiss Netflix, and (3) give me one question I can ask myself to tell paradigm-blindness apart from a genuinely sound skeptical prediction.

props.command: Those quotes from Keyes, Ellison, and Valenti all share the same trap — expertise from inside the domain. I want to pressure-test whether I'd have made the same call. Take today's loudest 'AI will fail' argument and (1) identify which specific domain expertise is fueling it, (2) show me how that same expertise would have made Blockbuster's CEO dismiss Netflix, and (3) give me one question I can ask myself to tell paradigm-blindness apart from a genuinely sound skeptical prediction.

beats.narration_text: The structure of the article is a list. Technology, year, quote, outcome. And the pattern is identical every time. An expert with real credentials makes a confident public prediction. The prediction is that a new technology has no future. The technology proceeds to reshape the economy. The expert is forgotten, or their company is gone, or they end up selling the thing they said was worthless. The list runs from 1925 to 2026. A century of wrong calls, made with complete certainty.

props.artifactTitle: The pattern — a century of wrong calls
artifactLines: 1943 — IBM chairman: 'world market for maybe five computers.' Now: billions.
1977 — DEC founder: 'no reason for anyone to have a computer at home.' DEC: gone.
1995 — Ethernet inventor: internet will 'catastrophically collapse in 1996.' Still here.
2007 — Microsoft CEO: iPhone has 'no chance' of significant market share. 2.3B sold.

beats.narration_text: A few of the quotes are worth sitting with. Jim Keyes, CEO of Blockbuster in 2008, on streaming: 'I've been frankly confused by this fascination that everybody has with Netflix.' Netflix passed three hundred million subscribers. Blockbuster has one store left. Larry Ellison, also 2008, on cloud computing: 'It's complete gibberish. It's insane. When is this idiocy going to stop?' The cloud became a seven-hundred-billion-dollar-a-year market. Oracle now sells thirty-four billion dollars of it annually. Jack Valenti, head of the MPAA, on home video: 'The VCR is to the American film producer as the Boston Strangler is to the woman home alone.' Within years, home video earned Hollywood more than the box office.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B02.png (screenshot of the article showing the Blockbuster/Netflix and Oracle/cloud quotes side by side, with their outcomes)

beats.narration_text: Why does this keep happening? The article implies but doesn't fully state the answer — so let's name it. These are not stupid people making careless predictions. They are domain experts making predictions from inside their own domain. The CEO of Blockbuster understood the physical video rental business better than almost anyone. That expertise was the problem. The better you understand the current model, the harder it is to see the replacement. The replacement doesn't look like an improvement to the existing model. It looks like a toy, or a distraction, or a technical curiosity.

artifactLines: Domain expertise makes you the best judge of the current model.
The replacement doesn't improve the current model — it replaces the whole frame.
Netflix looked wrong to Blockbuster because Blockbuster optimized for stores.
The new thing always looks like a toy until it doesn't.

beats.narration_text: Which brings us to AI. The article doesn't list specific AI skeptic quotes — it doesn't need to. The structure of the argument is that the current wave of skepticism about AI maps onto every previous wave of skepticism about transformative technology. The form is familiar: AI hallucinations, AI is overhyped, AI replaces no one, the productivity gains don't show up in the data. These may all be partially true right now, the way Netflix's early streaming quality was genuinely bad. The question is whether the trajectory matters more than the current state.

props.artifactTitle: The AI skeptic playbook — familiar form
props.artifactHeading: Current skeptic claims vs. historical pattern
artifactLines: 'AI hallucinates' → early Netflix streaming was also genuinely bad.
'No productivity gains yet' → internet's economic impact wasn't visible in 1997.
'It's just hype' → Blockbuster CEO also had real evidence for his position.
The historical bet: trajectory matters more than current state.

beats.narration_text: The underlying argument isn't that AI skeptics are stupid. It's that sitting out a technology wave because it's currently imperfect is the pattern of every institution that later had to scramble. The people who dismiss AI today are not different from the people who dismissed the internet, personal computers, or streaming. The cost of being wrong is asymmetric: if AI matters and you waited, you catch up from behind. If AI is overhyped and you learned it anyway, you lost some hours. The downside of learning is small. The downside of not learning is large.

artifactLines: AI matters + you waited: you catch up from behind, in a faster market.
AI overhyped + you learned it: you lost some hours and got a useful skill.
The downside of learning is small. The downside of dismissing is potentially large.
Historical base rate: confident expert dismissals have been wrong every time.
props.sparkLine: Learning AI is the low-risk bet.

beats.narration_text: One quote from the list deserves its own moment. 1941 — the British Medical Journal on penicillin: it 'does not appear to have been considered as possibly useful from any other point of view.' This was printed in a peer-reviewed journal. The discovery had already been made. The mechanism was understood. Penicillin has since saved an estimated two hundred million lives. The gatekeepers of knowledge, writing in the authoritative venue of their field, got the most important medical technology of the twentieth century completely wrong. Humility about predictions is not weakness. It's the lesson.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B06.png (screenshot of the article section showing the penicillin quote from the British Medical Journal with its outcome)

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Use this prompt to run the pattern in the other direction — instead of collecting wrong predictions about the past, collect what the current expert consensus is saying about AI, and stress-test it.

props.command: Find the 5 most commonly cited reasons AI will fail or is overhyped in 2026. For each one, tell me: (1) the strongest version of the argument, (2) the historical technology it most resembles, and (3) what it would take to prove it right vs. wrong. Be a fair referee.

props.title: AI will fail.
props.subline: They said that about everything else too
